sentences.text: Right.
We left everything what we had, our possessions.
We didn't have too much, but it was still things from home, what we had pictures and everything.
We just left it like this, and they took us to Plaszow.
Coming to Plaszow, it was like coming to hell again.
It was like a big, big jail or big city, which you see people walking around in the same outfits and barracks all over the place.
They took us to a-- they change our clothes.
And we had to leave everything behind, and they gave us some other clothes.
And because we came from Rabka-- this was a resort town-- they put us to the worst work in Plaszow.
This was the Steinbruch, which was the quarry.
It was right-- right near the villa where Goeth lived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, we had hard work in Rabka, but this was-- there was no comparison to what we had there.
And this was right after his windows.
And he was well-known.
He had to shoot everybody-- somebody in the morning before breakfast.
He was-- like Rosenbaum was a sadist, he was even-- he was a worse sadist, because he was just shooting people for no reason.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked very hard there.
We worked in-- during the day, and we slept for few hours a night.
Hard work, pulling stones.
I got injured from this.
I pulled-- I picked up once a big stone, and my back gave out.
I was lucky that somebody was there with me, picked me up, and they put me on the side that I should lay down.
But it was, it was-- the worst work was in Plaszow, was the Steinbruch.
We stayed there for about four weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the food was-- was also very bad in Plaszow.
Some people who had good positions helped themselves.
But we were new ones there.
We just came in, so-- so they took advantage of us.
We had barracks.
We slept in the morning-- during the-- it was at night there.
And somebody else slept during the day there, so there was not-- nothing yours.
You couldn't leave anything because there were night shifts and day shifts.
So the night shifts slept during the day, and the people who worked during the day slept there at night.
And there was a lager-- there was a barrack Alteste, like a kapo who was taking care of it.
And he was like-- like a guard, you know.
He did whatever he wanted.
If you did something wrong, he punished you, and-- and do all kind of bad things.
It was a very, a very-- the camp was horrible.
You were afraid to go somewhere because you didn't know what-- whom you could meet there.
And especially when you met Goeth or somebody, with his dogs or with things, you-- you were-- you were afraid you'd be torn apart.
We worked there in that camp very hard.
I remember one thing the kapo did.
It was a Jewish kapo from the Steinbruch.
And when lunchtime came, he gave us-- we got little tickets to get a lunch.
Each one got a little bit soup, a little watery soup.
You stayed in line for this and then you ate it up because you couldn't leave anything or somebody else would take it away from him.
So he gave me an extra ticket.
I should have two lunches.
I don't know, he felt sorry for me.
He had a few tickets.
So I went first, I stand in the line for my first lunch I-- with my little pot I had.
And I finished very fast, and I went back in the line.
It was-- and when I came to the door, there was a Jewish policeman standing, name was Green.
I remember his name.
He was heavy because he didn't-- he didn't suffer.
He didn't-- he-- they had plenty more to eat.
They were like SS men, the Jewish policeman there.
And he said he saw my face was red [INAUDIBLE], but he says, show me your spoon.
And my spoon was still wet from the first soup.
And he touched it.
He says, you had your soup already.
And he gave me such a [NON-ENGLISH] in my face that I was on the fifth step, and I fell down from the steps.
And he was a Jewish guy.
He wasn't a German.
And I didn't do anything wrong.
I had a ticket for it.
And from then on, he hit my ear.
I still have problems with my ear till now from then.
And it was-- it was hard working there.
We were in Plaszow, like, I would say about five months.
But the five months was like five years.
I see people, women, being dragged to-- and I saw graves with people, with blood on them where they're being shot.
We had to-- I-- we had to do all kind of work.
After we finished with the quarry, they took us out.
They put-- and now the new group came, so they put them there.
This was like a-- like a punishment for you.
We did all kind of work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, my kapo tells me, you are assigned to-- to take out the corpses from the ground and burn them, because when they shoot people, they used to shoot them and burn them.
And then came already in 1943, they were afraid that-- they didn't want to leave any-- any signs that they would shoot people.
So that's why they were burning them.
And I knew when I go to the work, I never come out of it because they don't want to have any witnesses left later.
I still had 500 zlotys with me.
I don't know how I hid it somewhere.
And I gave the guy 500 zloty It was a goyish kapo, not a Jew.
It was a goy.
And I said, see, here is 500 zloty.
See maybe you can take me out of that.
I was a young boy all by myself.
And he says, OK.
Let's see what I can do.
And in the morning, he says, OK, you don't have to go there anymore.
But people who went there-- what a work this was.
This was a smell that you smelled-- the people came into the barracks from that work.
I mean, you have to burn dead people to make ash of them.
They had to do it by hand and everything.
And I thought, I'll go there, I'll be finished.
I have felt that somebody was watching me all those years.

I-- you know, I forgot to mention it, can I go back to-- when I was in Rabka, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Rabka was, if you got sick, or if you couldn't work, you-- you'd be-- you-- automatically they shot you.
They put you on the list, and then next action they take you.
They took you away and you were finished.
I got some kind of a sickness.
I was-- I was dysentery.
You know what it is?
This is-- you went out with blood.
And it's a very catchy sickness, you know?
In other words, you have to be quarantined because other people would get sick.
Myself and another boy got sick on this.
And first, I was afraid to report it because I knew, in Rabka, if you sick, that's-- that's the end.
So they-- eventually, I had to go to the doctor because I was getting very weak.
So there was not a doctor, it was a guy who was a male nurse.
And he saw what it is, he says he reported it.
So the two of us, when they were going to work, we had to go separate.
And they took us on a truck and they brought us to the ghetto in Krakow to a hospital.
That friend of mine who had the same sickness, the next day he died.
He was a little bit older than I am.
And I was-- I was unconscious maybe for two days, and I came back to it.
And would you believe it, that they brought me back to Rabka from Pla-- from the ghetto, from the hospital?
And I heard that two or three days later, they liquidated the ghetto and they killed all the sick people.
I was feeling somebody was watching over me.
And in-- and in Plaszow, I was holding on as much as I could.
And then they needed a group.
Schindler was already having his own factory in the-- in the Zablocie, Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik.
Yeah.
I got it by Schindler.
I got it in Plaszow.
One day they-- they called-- my kapo told me they need a hundred people, night workers.
Schindler was building a-- was building a new building because he wanted to make a bigger camp to make-- bring more people.
So they were building a big people-- a big building, and they needed to finish it fast.
So they took a hundred people from Plaszow for night work, to work at night in building the big building.
We were lucky-- the whole our group went there.
I don't know how and what, because you use-- everything was mazel and [INAUDIBLE].
We came there to-- this was-- this was-- the Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik, was the outskirts of Plaszow.
But it belonged to Plaszow.
It was like you come in from hell to somewhere, you know, to [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you say it in English?
To-- Garden of Eden?
You know, there's a hell, and there is a--
Paradise, we come to a paradise.
Was a small place, and it was quiet, you know, not such a big thing like at Plaszow.
We went to work at night, and the food was better.
And we know what Schindler was.
You see, Schindler was trying to help the Jews to-- to survive.
That time, I didn't know anything about him.
He-- he was running a factory where they make pots, like you-- like you cook in the pots?
And also they made certain kind from missiles, little pieces for the army.
And that's what kept him going in the camp.
But unfortunately, nothing came out of it because the machines didn't work right.
So-- so-- but he had so much influence-- Schindler had so much influence in Germany that they let him keep the camp.
What we did there at night, we worked the whole night in-- in that-- [GERMAN] in the building, to build a big building for the factory.
And during the day, we slept.
But we had our own-- our own beds.
And there were bridges-- three-- three floors of bunks.
And the youngsters were always on the top bunk.
And we worked there.
One day, I was sitting after work in the bunk there, in the barrack, and I collapsed.
I fainted.
They called the nurse.
I remember there was-- there's the Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
I spoke to him.
He lives in Long Island.
And he had two nurses with him.
The nurse came over, and they found out I have pneumonia.
We had no hospital there in the-- in the Emalia.
There was just the laboratory for-- so they put me on my bed there.
And I was sick.
I stayed there like for three weeks.
And I couldn't eat anything.
My friend had my rationing.
He had double food every day, and I couldn't eat a thing.
I was-- and every night, the nurse came with the doctor and I had to come down from the bridge, from the bed, and they examined me.
Everybody was standing and looking, and I was like a skeleton.
I was very thin.
I don't know.
As I said, somebody was watching me, and I pulled through.
And then, I was so hungry because I was-- and I had nothing what to eat.
Because I only had my portion, which wasn't much.
I needed more to eat.
Sometimes I used to go to the kitchen for-- for seconds, but there was nothing there.
But God was good to me, and I survived that sickness too.
So I had already two-- two deadly sickness that I survive.
And I was-- I was working in the Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik there.
The camp was not as bad as any concentration camp.
Because we had better food to eat, and we were not afraid for the Germans-- for the Nazis.
Because even Goeth used to come, and Schindler was with him, he wouldn't touch anybody.
He had strict orders from Schindler not to touch.
And he said, out of the camp, you are the boss.
In the camp, I am the boss.
I remember one day I was going from-- from the barracks to the work, and here comes Schindler with Goeth.
And it was-- the ground was very muddy, was raining.
There was a lot of mud outside, and his boots were all dirty, Goeth's.
He saw me, he says, come here.
Clean my boots.
So I had-- with my finger, I cleaned my-- and I was lucky that Schindler was there, because if Schindler wasn't there, I would be finished with him.
Because he-- after whatever you do, he shot you.
So Schindler says, lauf.
Run away already.
This was my encounter with Goeth for the first time.
But you see, in Plaszow, in the Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik, Schindler was the boss.
So even the SS men who was watching us, they could not come to the camp to do anything bad to us.
So everybody was survived-- and everybody survived in the camp.
There were families there are from Krakow-- father, husband and wife.
And they had all easy work, because most of the people were from Krakow, so each-- everybody knew each other.
We were the newcomers.
We were from other towns, so we did all the heavy work.
They had the work in the kitchen.
One was a woman was cooking for people, you know, private foods.
Wasn't so bad.
But one day, we were sleeping in-- in the barracks.
And on our side, where we were in the barracks was an [? entlausen. ?] It means that they cleaned the barracks for lice and for everything.
So we had to move out from those barracks.
And we went-- we-- we went into the other barracks with the other people where they lived.
We were sleeping at night, and suddenly we heard a big noise and a fire.
What happened?
One of-- one of the English airplanes, a bomb airplane, fell down in our camp.
And it fell down on the barracks where we used to live.
And there was nobody there because they were-- they wanted to clean it up.
And there was such a fire and shooting from the plane that we didn't know what happened.
Everybody ran out in the-- at night, and we were running to the-- to the gate.
And the SS men were standing with their guns.
He says, you can't go out there.
We thought somebody put the lager on fire, and we're going to be all burned, till we found out.
And in the morning, was saw the plane was burned.
And the pilot was burned, sitting still on the-- on the-- on the seat with his-- with his thing there.
It was a English plane.
From then on, the camp was closed.
We had to go back to Plaszow every morning and walk back at-- at night, back to Plaszow.
It was back to the horror there.
And a lot of people-- they eliminated a lot of people to come down because they didn't need so many people.
That time Schindler was thinking about moving his factory to-- to Brunnlitz because the Russians were coming closer and closer.
And he bribed a lot of-- a lot of Germans.
He had a big push in Berlin, Schindler, and he was able to do whatever he wanted.
I happened to-- to get acquainted with him because I was working for somebody who, he used to come there.
But I met-- I know-- I knew him better later in Brunnlitz than in there.
So what happened?
We went back to Plaszow, and I didn't go anymore back to Emalia.
They put me to other work because they didn't need so many workers there.
So it was back to hell in the ma-- in the Plaszow again.
The same old story.
You had to fight for survival every day.
And you still saw all those shooting and beating and even worse.
They were sending people away to different camps because the Russians were coming closer and closer.
Yes, sure.
Well, we had to line up in a place where there was a special-- they called it Apellplatz.
And we had to-- they counted us.
We were counted, every camp, like jewels.
God forbid somebody was missing.
What happens in Plaszow when somebody ran away, they took the whole-- the whole barrack, and they shoot them.
So everybody was watching the other guy because you knew if you run away, there was nowhere to run.
But especially those commandos who went outside to work, outside of the camp.
There were a lot of commandos in Plaszow who went out to work outside.
And when they came back there to be counted, if somebody was missing, they took him straight from-- from the gate to the-- we used to call it a special-- a word for a mountain.
And there was like a ravine.
That's where they shoot the people, one after the other.
The one was responsible for the other.
Yes.
I was-- I was a witness to one of the thing, because we're working right next to the ravine.
And they brought-- they brought a whole group, young people.
And everyone was shot because one guy ran away.
So-- so the end was that everybody was responsible for the other guy.
So they had to watch everybody.
So if you wanted to save yourself, you endangered the whole group.
The whole group was like 30, 35 people.
So--
I would say about half an hour to an hour in the-- in the cold, in the snow, in the rain.
That-- there was no nothing.
We had to stay every-- every morning and every evening to be counted.
And then we went to our barracks.
You were exhausted.
You were hungry.
But this was life in camp.
You still-- the hope was that you survive.
You tried your best.
You-- you-- you went to steal food, and you knew if you'd be caught, you'd be-- you would be shot.
But you still went, because that's the only way you could survive.
We-- we called it-- to, you know, to-- there was a word for it, that-- to organize, you know?
We didn't say we're going to steal it.
We're going to organize.
So you stole-- but a potato was a big thing, or something like this.
And this the way only you survive.
You ate whatever you could.
If you-- we used to get a piece of bread.
You didn't leave it for the next day, because if you leave for the next day, it was not-- somebody will take it from you.
You sleep, and you take it from you.
I remember we used to get, in Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik, which was a really good camp, we used to get one bread for six people.
So the bread was like a-- it wasn't round.
It was long.
So you had to cut it into six pieces.
Everybody wanted the end, not the middle, because the end was more.
So how we did it, we cut it in six pieces.
And one had to turn around, you know, he didn't see it.
And he says, this is for Moshe.
This is for-- for whom is this?
This is for Yankel.
This is for-- this way was no-- you know, whoever luck it was.
And as soon as you got the bread, you ate it up because you couldn't leave it for the next day.
Somebody would knew that you had a piece bread.
It was like-- like animals.
It-- they took it away from me.
Everybody was for himself when it came to food.
I saw hanging in Rabka, and I saw hanging in Plaszow, too.
In Rabka, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a guy working for that Rosenbaum.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came in an SS man from another town, and told him he needs [pauses] the horse and wagon to bring him-- to-- to take him somewhere.
He didn't go to Rosenbaum and ask him, but he did it on his own.
He got so mad that he-- he says, you're going to be hanged.
The whole camp had to stay in front, and-- and he hung-- [INAUDIBLE] he fell down.
And there's a law.
If somebody falls down, you let him lie.
And [INAUDIBLE] and he-- was-- didn't help him.
You had to hang him.
And I saw it in Plaszow, too, hanging people for-- for no reason, for nothing.
It was that Human life was just nothing.
You just have to survive the best you could.
No, I got the tattoo in-- in-- at the-- we had to-- in the Deutsche Emailwarenfabrik, right.
We-- everybody had to be put a KL on it.
And I had an old doctor.
It was a Dr.
Lieberstein. ?] He didn't see so well, so he made it a little bit crooked-- [LAUGHS] That's why I'm laughing now.
Everybody-- after they made the tattoo, everybody tried to suck it out.
But still, from the L here, one piece is missing.
But I will never get rid of it.
You see, I was working here in America for a big company.
They were German Jews.
And one of the presidents, he says, you know, I'll-- I'll pay for it to take it out.
Do you make it plastic surgery?
I said, why?
I will never take it out.
I didn't get it so easy.
Why should I take it out?
But I always wear long sleeves.
I never, even in the summer, I never wear short sleeves.
My-- my grandchildren used to ask me what it is.
But when-- as they-- as they get older, I tell them.
When they're little kids, we can't tell them what it is.
I said, oh, it's just some kind of initials, I used to tell them.
But you know, you used to go-- in the beginning, I used to wear short sleeve.
In the subway, people used to look then.
I decided, well, I have to show it.
So I wear long sleeve, and I got to the habit that I always wear long sleeves.
OK.
From Plaszow, we went to Gross Rosen.
There was a whole story before I went to Gross Rosen.
Schindler [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wanted to take-- wanted to take his factory and move it to Czechoslovakia, to Brunnlitz, which was the [INAUDIBLE] in the area.
Now, everybody wanted to go get on that list.
He had a thousand people who wanted that list-- who wanted to be-- I mean, he wanted to take thousand people.
And what he wanted to do, to take is all the people from Emalia, where we worked for him, he wants to take them.
Those are his people.
We were being called Schindler's-- Schindler's people.
And as soon as the people, the [NON-ENGLISH], the police, the Jewish police, found out that they were going to be transported, they wanted to be on the list.
And there was a guy, Marcel Goldberg.
He was an alderman, and he took money from people.
They had money, they had diamonds, they had gold, still, in Plaszow, to put them on the list and took out other people who were supposed to go.
One day, we were standing in Apellplatz, and they were taking-- calling the names for these people to go to Brunnlitz.
If you-- you knew if you go to Brunnlitz, you-- you may have a chance to survive.
Because the rest of the people were sent to-- to other camps-- Dachau, and they were sent to all kind of extermination camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't know anybody.
So I was, you know, I was not from Krakow, from the group.
I was just there from-- but there was one man that he knew me.
He owned the factory of of-- where Schindler took over the factory.
His name was Abraham Banker.
He took over the factory from Schindler-- from-- Schindler took over the factory from him.
And he-- he was a religious man, that Banker, before the war.
And in camp, he tried to keep as much as you could.
And he knew a little bit about me, that I was in camp, and I was-- I used to daven.
He shown me once, we had not film, but we-- sometimes we borrowed film.
We used to daven.
And they were calling people, like a thousand people, and it-- I was left with few people already.
Because whoever called in went down to the group there.
And then they called my name, too.
I was almost maybe five of the list want to be called.
It's like, you know, like a new-- new way of life.
You get a new certificate of life if you heard your name being called.
So I knew I'm going with Schindler, and I knew I'm going to be protected, because he wanted to save the people.
This was his-- his aim, to save the people.
I don't know what people talk-- what he did, but he was an angel.
He must have been sent from God.
Because how could he-- he was-- he was an SS man before.
He joined the party.
But then he saw what the Germans were doing to the Jews.
He had a change-- change of-- of thinking, what they do to them.
So he wanted to save as many Jews as he can.
And he, not once, he risked his own life to-- to save a Jew.
So we-- the whole group went on cattle cars.
We went to Gross Rosen.
And Gross Rosen was an extermination camp.
It was-- was-- everything where we went was worse than the other one.
It was worse than Plaszow.
They took us in-- in the front of the [? entlausen ?] where they are-- want to clean us up.
We had to leave our clothes here on one side and go into the door, and then they-- we went to showers.
We didn't know where we going.
We went to showers, and then we went out the other door.
And whatever we had possession from before, it was gone.
The give us just-- just the long sleeping gowns to wear.
And it was in November.
It was cold.
